summary:
The Taiping Heavenly Kingdom (1850–1864 AD) was one of the deadliest civil wars in human history, a millenarian Christian-inspired rebellion against the Qing dynasty that at its peak controlled much of southern China and claimed tens of millions of lives. Its founder, Hong Xiuquan — a failed Confucian examination candidate who believed himself to be the younger brother of Jesus Christ — established a theocratic state centered at Nanjing (renamed the Heavenly Capital) with radical social policies: communal property, prohibition of opium and foot-binding, and nominal gender equality. The Taiping vision fused Protestant Christian imagery absorbed from missionaries with traditional Chinese millenarian traditions, producing a distinctly syncretic ideology that was simultaneously revolutionary and deeply Chinese.

The rebellion's scale was staggering: an estimated 20–30 million people died from combat, famine, and disease — more than in any conflict before the 20th century. The Qing dynasty, whose weakness the rebellion exposed, only suppressed it through a combination of Han Chinese regional armies led by figures like Zeng Guofan and foreign-led mercenary forces (the 'Ever Victorious Army' commanded by Frederick Townsend Ward and later Charles Gordon). The suppression of the Taiping Rebellion accelerated the shift of real military power from Manchu Bannermen to Han Chinese regional commanders — a decentralization that would contribute to the dynasty's eventual collapse in 1912. The rebellion also confirmed European powers' assessment of Qing weakness, intensifying foreign pressure on China in the late 19th century.
